aliases: Deep-fried tofu puff braised in dark soy sauce

english: Deep-fried tofu puff braised in dark soy sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised taupok (deep-fried tofu puff stewed in dark soy sauce); closest match is the general tofu food product class. Braised preparation and deep-fried puff form are not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soybean curd (tofu) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised taupok; taupok is a deep-fried puffed form of tofu subsequently braised in soy-based sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for taupok or braised tofu puff. A00LC (soybean curd/tofu) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised taupok or tofu puff as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised taupok is approximately 80g, equivalent to 3–4 braised tofu puff pieces as served in a hawker centre side dish or as part of a mixed rice meal.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Taupok — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Taupok (F09050003). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical entry. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central tofu puff reference (NDB 16426) as approximately 2.5g/100g for deep-fried tofu puff, adjusted for braising context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Soy-based foods GI reference; tofu GI analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised taupok in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 32 based on the closest analogous food: plain tofu / soy-based foods, which consistently show GI values in the 15–40 range in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021; Foster-Powell et al., 2002). The very low carbohydrate content (3.9g/100g) means glycemic load per 80g serving is approximately 1.0, confirming negligible glycemic impact. Braising in dark soy sauce adds minimal carbohydrate. Estimate is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 403mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension suitability
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit braised taupok to small portions (≤50g) and avoid consuming the braising sauce, as sodium at 403mg/100g exceeds safe thresholds; use low-sodium soy sauce alternatives when preparing at home.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.9g/100g), zero sugar, and low glycemic index (~32) result in negligible postprandial blood glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised taupok is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.9g/100g), zero sugar, and estimated low GI of approximately 32; however, portion size should be moderated given the high fat content (17.5g/100g) contributing to caloric density, and braising sauce ingredients should be confirmed to contain no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.5g/100g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium (403mg/100g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume braised taupok in moderation: the deep-frying process increases total fat to 17.5g/100g and sodium at 403mg/100g elevates blood pressure risk; soy protein (7.5g/100g) and calcium (139mg/100g) provide modest cardioprotective benefit, but portion control and low-sodium preparation are strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium also exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Braised taupok is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because the deep-frying process generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and sodium at 403mg/100g contributes to tissue swelling; substituting with non-fried firm tofu braised in low-sodium sauce is a clinically preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised taupok is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of deep-fried soya beancurd puffs (taupok) that have been slowly stewed in a savoury dark soy sauce-based braising liquid. Taupok refers to the hollow, spongy tofu puffs created by deep-frying firm tofu at high temperature, which causes the exterior to become golden and crispy while the interior develops a porous, absorbent texture ideal for soaking up braising sauces. The braising liquid typically contains dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, sugar, garlic, five spice powder, and water, imparting a rich, umami-forward, slightly sweet and savoury flavour profile. The finished dish has a soft, yielding texture with a deeply coloured exterior. Nutritionally, braised taupok provides 203 kcal per 100g, with a high fat content of 17.5g (predominantly from the deep-frying process), moderate protein of 7.5g from soy, very low carbohydrate of 3.9g, and zero sugar. It is a notable source of calcium (139mg/100g) and iron (2.7mg/100g), reflecting the mineral content of soy. Sodium is elevated at 403mg/100g due to the soy sauce braising medium, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Braised taupok is commonly served as a side dish in hawker centres, as part of mixed rice (cai fan) selections, or as a component of lor mee and other braised noodle dishes across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised taupok?
Is braised taupok suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of braised taupok?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry F09050003 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central tofu puff reference. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous soy food data. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for braised taupok as a composite dish are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F09050003 or in the USDA FoodData Central tofu puff entries as of 2026. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F09050003); this reflects the braising sauce formulation used in the HPB reference preparation. Saturated_fat_g of 2.5g/100g is an approved-source estimate from USDA FoodData Central tofu puff (NDB 16426) adapted for braised preparation context and documented accordingly.
